description:
A junior backup-operations engineer has asked you, the senior on-call, for a quick
  integrity check of last night’s SQL dumps.  
  Three compressed dumps already exist in  

      /home/user/data/backups  

  and are named in strict UTC-timestamp form:

      backup-YYYYMMDDThhmmssZ.sql

  Your goal is to verify, in *your own local time zone* (Asia/Kolkata, UTC+05:30),
  whether each dump is less than 24 hours old.  
  The engineer also needs the desktop monitoring system to show the results in a
  single, machine-parseable log file so that Grafana can ingest it.

  What you have to do
  -------------------
  1. Work strictly inside your normal user account; you do **not** have root.
  2. Temporarily switch the shell environment so every command in this session
     obeys:
        • Time zone  : Asia/Kolkata  
        • Locale     : en_US.UTF-8
     (Do **not** touch /etc/locale.conf or /etc/localtime; use environment
     variables only.)
  3. Read every file whose name matches the
     “backup-YYYYMMDDThhmmssZ.sql” pattern in /home/user/data/backups.
     Derive the UTC timestamp from the **file name** (not the mtime).  
     Convert that timestamp to Asia/Kolkata local time.
  4. Decide the “freshness” status:  
        • OK     – the local timestamp is < 24 h old compared with “now”  
        • STALE  – 24 h old or more
  5. Create the directory (if absent)

        /home/user/backup_verification

     and within it create/overwrite  

        last_backup.log
  6. Write one log line per dump in **ascending UTC order** using *exactly* this
     pipe-delimited format (no extra spaces):

        <filename>|<utc_iso>|<local_iso>|<status>

     • <filename>     – the exact file name, e.g. backup-20240117T231500Z.sql  
     • <utc_iso>       – UTC in ISO-8601 *with* trailing “Z”, e.g.
                           2024-01-17T23:15:00Z  
     • <local_iso>     – Asia/Kolkata in ISO-8601 *with* “+05:30”, e.g.
                           2024-01-18T04:45:00+05:30  
     • <status>        – OK or STALE (all caps)

  7. After the per-file lines, append **one** summary line:

        LATEST BACKUP: <filename> <status>

     where <filename> is the newest backup (largest UTC timestamp) and
     <status> is its evaluated freshness.

  8. Display the final contents of last_backup.log on screen so the engineer can
     copy-paste it into Slack.

  Formatting rules that the automated test will enforce
  -----------------------------------------------------
  • Log file path must be exactly  
        /home/user/backup_verification/last_backup.log  
  • Every data line must match this regular expression  
        ^backup-[0-9]{8}T[0-9]{6}Z\.sql\|
         [0-9]{4}-[0-9]{2}-[0-9]{2}T[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}Z\|
         [0-9]{4}-[0-9]{2}-[0-9]{2}T[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}\+05:30\|
         (OK|STALE)$
  • Summary line must match  
        ^LATEST BACKUP: backup-[0-9]{8}T[0-9]{6}Z\.sql (OK|STALE)$
  • The file must end with a single trailing newline.

  Expected difficulty
  -------------------
  A competent Linux user can finish this with 5–10 shell commands
  (export, ls/printf/awk/date/sort/etc.).  No scripting language beyond POSIX
  shell is required, but you may write a short shell script if you prefer.

  Notes & tips
  ------------
  • `date -d '<UTC-string>' +%s` is handy, as is `TZ=Asia/Kolkata date -d ...`.  
  • Make sure to *export* TZ and LANG so that child processes (like awk) inherit
    them.  
  • Watch out for leading zeros when converting times.  ISO-8601 demands them.  
  • The agent must create the directory if it does not exist and must overwrite
    any pre-existing last_backup.log.  
  • You do **not** need to read or decompress the SQL files; only examine their
    names.

  Once done, confirm that:
      cat /home/user/backup_verification/last_backup.log
  prints the correctly formatted output described above.
truth:
<!-- Pre-seeded backup files that already exist before the agent starts -->
  Required existing directory:
      /home/user/data/backups

  Required existing files inside that directory:
      /home/user/data/backups/backup-20240115T231500Z.sql
      /home/user/data/backups/backup-20240116T231500Z.sql
      /home/user/data/backups/backup-20240117T231500Z.sql

  (These are zero-byte placeholder files; only the file names matter.)

  Verification logic the grader will follow:
      1. Confirm that /home/user/backup_verification/last_backup.log exists.
      2. Read the log file line by line:
         • For every line except the last, ensure it matches the data-line regex
           described in the public task.
         • Save the last data line’s <filename>; that is the expected “latest”.
      3. Verify the final line matches the summary regex and that the embedded
         <filename> equals the one kept from step 2.
      4. For every <utc_iso> token, check it equals the canonical conversion of
         the timestamp embedded in <filename> (ISO-8601 with trailing “Z”).
      5. For every <local_iso>, check it equals the <utc_iso> converted from UTC
         to Asia/Kolkata (+05:30) in ISO-8601 form.
      6. For each line, recompute freshness (current epoch - local epoch) and
         ensure the stated OK/STALE value is correct to within ±2 seconds.
      7. Ensure the file ends with exactly one trailing newline and no carriage
         returns.

  Grader ignores screen output; only the log file is authoritative.
